title: Blessed Are the Merciful
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Blessed are the Merciful - Pastor Tito Lyro - Matthew 5_7.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Movement of the Beatitudes
subpoints: The Beatitudes were chosen this week because of the explosion in Beirut and the merciful response of Lebanese Christians—mercy is needed now
The Beatitudes first draw us into ourselves (poor in spirit, mourning, meekness) and then drive us outward toward God and others
After poverty of spirit, mourning over sin, and meekness, we hunger and thirst for God's righteousness (Matthew 6:33)
As we are filled with God's righteousness, we turn to others in mercy—and as we are merciful, we receive mercy

outline.title: The Mercy We Have Received: God's Mercy in Christ
subpoints: It is only those who have found the mercy of God in salvation who can be truly merciful to others—mercy flows from grace received
Jesus is described in Hebrews as the merciful high priest who was made like us in every way (yet without sin) in order to help those who are tempted
God's mercy in Christ helps us to our feet when we have fallen; it pours the oil of healing into the wounds of conscience, memory, and personality
God's mercy is inexhaustible: 'The mercy of the LORD is from everlasting to everlasting to those who fear Him' (Psalm 103:17)—you can never use it all up

outline.title: What Is Mercy? The Good Samaritan (Luke 10:30-37)
subpoints: The best illustration of mercy is the parable of the good Samaritan—asked who was the neighbor, Jesus answers: he who showed mercy
Mercy relieves the consequences of sin in the lives of others, whether they have been sinned against or have sinned themselves
The Samaritan took responsibility for the injured man—ministering to his broken body, providing for restoration and healing—without any obligation to do so
The Samaritan did not chase the robbers, did not protest societal failure, did not complain about systemic injustice—he saw the immediate need and met it

outline.title: Jesus as the Good Samaritan: Our Pattern for Mercy
subpoints: The early church identified the good Samaritan with Jesus Himself—He is the one who finds the bruised and broken and heals them
When Jesus encountered those who were bruised, He did not break them; when He found dimly burning wicks, He fanned them into flame
Isaiah's prophecy quoted in Matthew 12: the Messiah will not break a bruised reed or quench a smoking flax—this is the pattern for our ministry
Our nation should see us as followers of Jesus Christ who are merciful, who do not break the bruised or extinguish the struggling

outline.title: Why We Fail to Show Mercy: The Priest and the Levite
subpoints: Both the priest and the Levite were covenant members of God's people—Jesus is describing behavior found in the church, not just among 'bad people'
The priest and Levite doubtless had their reasons: inconvenience, their own plans, the risk of ceremonial uncleanness from touching a potentially dead body
Jesus did not think their ordinary routines should trump being merciful—and neither does He think our ordinary routines should trump mercy
It is better to run the risk of becoming ceremonially unclean than to fail to show mercy; it is not a sin to be ceremonially unclean, but it is always a sin to be unmerciful

outline.title: Application: Being Merciful Because We Have Received Mercy
subpoints: Being merciful is the natural result of receiving Christ and experiencing the grace of God—if you are truly a believer, you cannot help but be merciful
As you are merciful, you experience the final mercy of God in the day of judgment; those not merciful have reason to question the genuineness of their faith
People of God, mercy is getting down on your hands and knees and doing what you can to restore dignity to someone whose life has been broken by sin
Have you ever stopped for the sake of the bruised and broken, or have you always found reason to pass by on the other side?

summary:
This sermon on Matthew 5:7 was prompted by the explosion in Beirut and the remarkable mercy shown by Lebanese Christians in its aftermath. Pastor Tito Lyro uses the occasion to consider the fifth beatitude: "Blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy." He traces the internal logic of the Beatitudes—from poverty of spirit, to mourning, to meekness, to hungering and thirsting for righteousness—showing how the Beatitudes first drive us inward to see our own emptiness before turning us outward in mercy toward others.

The sermon distinguishes two movements that must be held together: the mercy God has poured on us, and the mercy we are to show others. We can only be merciful to others because God has been merciful to us in Christ the merciful high priest who was made like us in every way, bore our temptations fully, and still pours inexhaustible mercy on those who come to Him. Psalm 103:17 and Lamentations 3 are cited to show that God's mercy is from everlasting to everlasting—it can never be exhausted.

The sermon then defines mercy through the parable of the good Samaritan (Luke 10:30-37): mercy relieves the consequences of sin in the lives of others. The early church identified the good Samaritan with Jesus Himself, and Pastor Lyro follows that interpretation, showing how Jesus healed the bruised and fanned the flickering wick to flame, and how this is to be our pattern. The sermon is honest about why we fail to show mercy: inconvenience, our own plans, and various rationalizations—noting that the priest and Levite were covenant members of God's people, not villains. The sermon closes with the challenge: have you stopped for the bruised and broken, or have you passed by on the other side?
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Matthew 5:1-12

And seeing the multitudes, He went up on a mountain, and when He was seated His disciples came to Him. Then He opened His mouth and taught them, saying: Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted. Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be filled. Blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God. Blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness' sake, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are you when they revile and persecute you, and say all kinds of evil against you falsely for My sake. Rejoice and be exceedingly glad, for great is your reward in heaven, for so they persecuted the prophets who were before you.

## Sermon

### Introduction: Why This Beatitude, Why Now

I am going to skip two beatitudes today and drop to verse 7. I will go back to the others—they are great and we cannot leave them uncovered. But because of events in the world this week—the explosion that took place in Beirut and the response of the Christians in Lebanon—I thought it would do us well to consider the place of mercy in our lives, the mercy that God has for us, and the mercy that we are to show because God has been merciful to us.

### The Movement of the Beatitudes

If you read through the Beatitudes, you will notice that they first draw us into ourselves and then bring us out of ourselves. They start with the idea of being poor in spirit—looking at our own hearts and seeing how we have nothing to give to the Lord. Then they move to mourning over our sins. Then to meekness as God works humility in us and we turn to Him. Then, after driving us into ourselves, the Beatitudes turn us outward. Once we have become poor in spirit and have mourned over our sins, we hunger and thirst after God's righteousness—seeking God's kingdom first (Matthew 6:33).

As we hunger and thirst for God's righteousness, He fills us. And as we do that, we also turn to others in mercy. And as we are merciful, we receive God's mercy. We deal with others with cleansed hearts and become peacemakers. And we rejoice when we suffer for the testimony of Jesus Christ.

The Scriptures are a mirror that portray back to us exactly who we are. When we look at them we see that we are dead in our trespasses and sins, that in us (that is, in the flesh) no good thing dwells. And when that happens, we become poor in spirit. We mourn over our sins. We become meek before God. And after driving us into ourselves, the Spirit of Jesus drives us out of ourselves and we are enabled to hunger and thirst after God's own righteousness—fully assured that we are going to be satisfied.

### The Mercy We Have Received

It is only those who have found the mercy of God in salvation who can truly be merciful to others. That is exactly what Jesus says in verse 7: "Blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy." This does not mean we earn mercy by being merciful—it means being merciful is the natural result of receiving Christ and experiencing the grace of God. If you are truly a believer, you cannot help but be merciful.

Do you know the mercy of God? In Hebrews 2:18, Jesus is described as the merciful high priest. In order to become this type of high priest, Jesus was made like us in every single way, yet without sin. Because He Himself suffered when He was tempted, He is able to help those who are being tempted. He knows what it is like to be us because He has experienced what we go through. In fact, because He withstood every temptation without falling, He experienced the full strength of Satan's attack in its unbroken intensity. And He did that in order to help those who are tempted.

God's mercy in Christ helps us to our feet when we have fallen. It defends and protects us when we are weak and helpless. God pours His mercy upon us. God's mercy pours the oil of healing into the wounds of our conscience, our heart, our memories, our personality. God's mercy supplies the place of rest and encouragement we need in the fellowship of His people. And at the last day, this same God will bring final healing and restoration when He transforms us into the image of God at the return of Christ, making body and soul perfect to be able to fellowship with Him.

And the mercy of the Lord is inexhaustible. You can never exhaust it. Psalm 103:17: "The mercy of the LORD is from everlasting to everlasting to those who fear Him." As the hymn says, "Great is Thy faithfulness." Do you ever feel that you have sinned one too many times and God is not going to forgive you again? Be assured that is never the case. If you believe in Jesus Christ, the mercy of God is never ever exhausted. You can go forever and God still has mercy to pour upon you.

### What Is Mercy? The Good Samaritan (Luke 10:30-37)

Let us turn to Luke 10. Jesus illustrates for us what it means to be merciful. A certain man went down from Jerusalem to Jericho and fell among thieves who stripped him, wounded him, and departed, leaving him half dead. A priest came down the road and when he saw him, passed by on the other side. A Levite came and looked and passed by. But a certain Samaritan as he journeyed came where the man was, and when he saw him he had compassion. He went to him, bandaged his wounds, poured on oil and wine, set him on his own animal, brought him to an inn, and took care of him. The next day he gave the innkeeper two denarii and said, "Take care of him, and whatever more you spend, when I come again I will repay you." Jesus asks: "Which of these three do you think was neighbor to him who fell among the thieves?" The answer: he who showed mercy on him. "Go and do likewise."

The parable was given as an answer to the lawyer's question about who one's neighbor is. And in verse 36 Jesus asks who fulfilled the law by being a neighbor—loving the man as himself. The answer: he who showed mercy. The Samaritan illustrates the meaning of mercy.

Two things stand out about the Samaritan as an example of mercy. First, mercy relieves the consequences of sin in the lives of others—whether they have been sinned against or have sinned themselves. In one sense, mercy is giving someone what they don't deserve. The Samaritan took responsibility for the injured man, ministered to his broken and bruised body, and did everything he could to provide for restoration and healing—without any obligation to do so. He didn't deal with the cause of the man's need by chasing the robbers. He didn't complain about the failure of society. He didn't stop to protest. The need was right there and he went and ministered to the man.

### Jesus as the Good Samaritan

The early church often identified the good Samaritan with Jesus. What Jesus is telling you in this parable is a parable about Himself. When Jesus encountered those who were bruised, He did not break them. When Jesus met people whose lives were like dimly burning wicks, He fanned them into flame. Jesus restored the weak and the bruised. He never passed by people who were suffering without ministering to them.

That is why Matthew tells us in chapter 12 that the prophecy of Isaiah—"He will not break a bruised reed or quench a smoking flax"—is a description of Jesus' ministry. And that is a description of our ministry as followers of Jesus Christ. Our nation should see us as those who are merciful, not those who break the bruised and extinguish the struggling.

### Why We Fail to Show Mercy: The Priest and the Levite

Have you ever stopped for the sake of the bruised and broken? Or have you always found reason to pass by on the other side?

Notice that Jesus is not describing the behavior of really bad people out there. Both the Levite and the priest were covenant members of God's people—this is a description of the behavior of God's people, of people in the church.

The priest and the Levite who passed by the wounded man doubtless had their reasons. Perhaps they didn't have time for this right now. Perhaps if they stopped they might touch a dead body and become ceremonially unclean, and then they couldn't get on with their ordinary routine. They had all kinds of good reasons. The same type of reasons we might have.

But Jesus really didn't think that their ordinary routines should trump being merciful. And Jesus really doesn't think that our ordinary routines should trump our being merciful. Mercy does not hide behind unbiblical reservations in order to protect itself from costly service. It is better to run the risk of becoming ceremonially unclean than to fail to show mercy to the needy. It is not a sin to be ceremonially unclean. It is always a sin to be unmerciful.

### Application: Because We Have Received Mercy, We Must Be Merciful

People of God, mercy is getting down on your hands and knees and doing what you can to restore dignity to someone whose life has been broken by sin—either his own sin or the sins done to him.

Being merciful is the natural result of receiving Christ and experiencing the grace of God. If you are truly a believer, you cannot help but be merciful. And as you are merciful, you experience the final mercy of God in the day of judgment. To be a Christian and not to be merciful is an impossibility.

So I need to ask you: do you know the mercy of God? Do you know the mercy of God in Jesus Christ? If you come to believe in Jesus, the merciful one who came to share in our weakness, God has been merciful to you. Because we have experienced God's mercy, we must be merciful. It is only true of us if we have first found the mercy of God in Christ.

And now all that I have said about being merciful to others is very important—but it is only true, it is only important, based on what I said first: the mercy of God. People of one generation tend to emphasize the mercy of God; people of another tend to emphasize being merciful to others. Both go together. We are merciful because God has been merciful to us—keep those together always.
